story:
Greg watches police raid his grandmother’s apartment and pin him to the wall while they force Lita, his precious grandmother, to kneel and place her shriveled hands on her head. They ransack her little altar, treat her candles and sacred images as junk, and even crush the head of a Virgin Mary statue. Shame washes over him; Lita has only ever tried to raise him after heroin took his parents, and now she bears his disgrace. One considerate officer helps her to a chair, but his kindness stops at Greg.

In the patrol car, icy rain streaks the window as he sees Lita at the building entrance, blocked by a burly officer. The rain makes the scene look like tears. He cannot summon his usual swagger. He feels his dignity evaporate and, for the first time, sees his grandmother as poor and betrayed.

The case is simple: he was caught with twenty-one crack vials, a repeat offense. Three years. He thinks, bitterly, seven vials a year. He pretends not to care, but his heart is full of holes. He frets: who will help Lita with groceries, with everything? As the bailiff moves him toward the door all prisoners hate, he spots Lita entering the courtroom. He calls, begs for a goodbye kiss. No one lets him. Judgment stands.

Nineteen, he lands in solitary after fighting to stay free of predatory prison gangs. A gorgeous orange sunset glows through the grimy window. It is his birthday, and he wants to die. A single phone call with Lita had revealed a new weakness in her voice that gnaws at him. He cannot stand the way some inmates seem unafraid to waste away in jail, even eager to repeat the cycle. The sunset’s beauty makes the cell feel smaller. He tries to think nothing, to stop the clock in his head, when the slot in the door slides open.

Givens, the unit supervisor, says, “Happy birthday,” and passes him a red-velvet cupcake with an unlit candle. Then, kindly but firm, he asks, “What are you doing here? You’re too smart for this place.”

The question cracks something open. In a rush Greg relives the day, at five years old, he ran from a nightmare into a worse one: his parents dead from heroin, small bags on the dresser, a blood-tipped needle on his mother’s chest, his own screams echoing until uniforms broke the door. The funeral. Lita’s lap. He remembers how he embraced school and her love—until Nelson lured him with fast money and street respect. Lie by lie, he traded her sacrifices for back-alley deals, then the arrest in her home, the confusion in her eyes. He had love and threw it away.

“I know why I’m here,” he says at last. “Now I don’t know what to do.”

“Change,” Givens tells him. “Make a vow. Start with yourself. Is there anyone you care about?”

Memories of Lita flood in: her smoothing his communion suit; the tembleque she made when he did his homework; her proud face as he recited Frost—“and miles to go before I sleep.” He says he loves his grandmother. That’s enough, Givens says. When Greg asks for help, the guard lays out steps: get him out of solitary and transferred off B Block, away from the gangs and the trouble, try for kitchen duty, sign him up for G.E.D. classes, then the new College Annex Program with real credits. But it’s on Greg. Most people make wishes on their birthdays; he needs to make a vow.

Givens leaves. Holding the cupcake, Greg hears the retreating footsteps and feels, for the first time in months, not just the desperate wish to get out, but a pull toward a different life—one worthy of Lita. He looks at the unlit candle and, through tears, makes his vow. “Happy birthday to me,” he whispers, and means it.
